content.text: Upgrading an LLM SDK or switching to a new model version is not a routine dependency bump. The LLM provider is the core reasoning engine of an agentic AI system, and any change to the model or client library can alter agent behaviour in ways that are invisible to standard regression suites: different tool-call selection patterns, changed response formatting, altered instruction-following characteristics, or new error response shapes. A systematic upgrade program manages this risk by combining a monitored model inventory, structured deprecation tracking, eval-driven quality comparison, and a disciplined deployment process.

An LLM SDK and model version upgrade program is the set of processes, tooling, and policies that govern how a team monitors available SDK and model versions, evaluates the impact of upgrading, and executes upgrades safely. It covers three distinct upgrade types: SDK version upgrades (new versions of the anthropic, openai, or litellm Python library), model version upgrades (upgrading from one model version to another, e.g., from claude-3-haiku to claude-3-5-haiku), and model deprecation migrations (migrating away from a model version that the provider is sunsetting).

The program is built around a model inventory: a YAML file that tracks every model version in use, its provider, the pinned SDK version, the use cases it serves, its GA date, its end-of-support date (if announced), and the planned upgrade target. The inventory is the source of truth for what the system is running and what needs to change. Monitoring the inventory against deprecation announcements and SDK release notes drives the upgrade backlog.

For each upgrade type, the program specifies which tests must pass before deployment: SDK upgrades require API schema compatibility checks and the full eval suite; model version upgrades require an A/B evaluation comparing the current and candidate model on the production task distribution; deprecation migrations follow the same process as model version upgrades but with a hard deadline.

LLM providers release new model versions and deprecate old ones on roughly 12-18 month cycles. Without a systematic upgrade program, teams face one of two failure modes: they fall behind on model versions, accumulating technical debt as older models approach end-of-support and the team scrambles to migrate before the forced sunset date; or they upgrade reactively, with insufficient evaluation, and discover quality regressions in production that affect user experience.

The SDK upgrade problem is more subtle. The anthropic Python SDK has shipped breaking changes in minor version bumps: response field renaming, stop_reason type changes from string to enum, streaming event schema changes. A team that does not track SDK release notes and compare response schemas before upgrading may not notice the regression until a production incident surfaces it — the agent stops calling tools correctly, or the streaming response parser throws exceptions on an unexpected event type.

Model deprecation is the most predictable challenge: providers announce end-of-support dates 6-12 months in advance. A team with a deprecation monitoring system and a clear migration process will upgrade before the deadline on their own schedule. A team without one will upgrade under pressure at the last minute, with less evaluation rigor and higher risk.
Tracking EOS dates 90+ days in advance converts forced last-minute migrations into planned upgrades with adequate evaluation time.
Requiring an A/B eval comparison before every model version upgrade ensures that quality does not regress without the team's explicit awareness and approval.
API schema compatibility checks before every SDK upgrade catch response format changes before they reach production.
The eval comparison captures token usage per case, enabling a cost-per-task comparison between the current and candidate model before committing to the upgrade.
The model inventory and eval comparison results constitute a documented record of which model versions have been evaluated and why each upgrade was approved or deferred.

Running the full eval suite against two models (current and candidate) doubles the evaluation cost. For large eval suites, this may take 30-60 minutes and cost tens of dollars in API fees per evaluation run.

An eval suite that does not cover the full production task distribution may miss regressions in underrepresented task types. Coverage gaps are only discovered when the task that wasn't covered in the eval surfaces a regression in production.

If the team's eval suite reveals that the successor model is not yet quality-comparable to the current model, but the current model has an imminent EOS date, the team faces a difficult choice: upgrade despite quality concerns or negotiate an extension with the provider.
The model inventory requires active maintenance as new models are added and deprecation dates are announced. Without a clear owner, the inventory goes stale.
The upgrade program operates through four parallel activities: monitoring, evaluation, deployment, and lifecycle tracking.

Monitoring: a weekly review of LLM provider release notes (Anthropic changelog, OpenAI release notes, etc.) and SDK GitHub releases identifies new versions that may warrant evaluation. A daily deprecation monitor checks the model inventory for models approaching EOS dates and creates alerts at 90, 60, 30, and 14 days before the migration deadline.

Evaluation: when an upgrade is identified, the triage decision determines the track (emergency for CVEs, planned window for breaking changes, defer for low-priority bumps). Planned upgrades run the A/B evaluation comparing current and candidate, the API schema compatibility check, and the full eval suite. The evaluation produces a structured comparison report with pass rate delta, latency delta, cost delta, and a deployment recommendation.

Deployment: approved upgrades follow the scheduled upgrade window process (deploy to staging, run eval on staging, canary deploy to 5% of production, monitor for 30 minutes, full rollout). The model inventory is updated with the new pinned version after successful deployment.

Lifecycle tracking: the deprecation monitor runs daily and reports approaching deadlines. The model inventory is reviewed in the monthly capacity review meeting to ensure that all models in use have current deprecation status and that migrations in progress are on track.

attrs.alt: LLM SDK and model version upgrade decision flow: monitoring signals feed triage decision, leading to emergency/planned/defer tracks, with eval validation gate before deployment

content.text: Input signals (provider changelogs, SDK releases, deprecation notices, CVE alerts) feed a triage decision that routes upgrades to the emergency track, planned window, or deferral. Planned upgrades pass through an eval validation gate comparing current and candidate models before deployment is approved.

This upgrade program applies to any team with at least one LLM model in production use. The full program (inventory, deprecation monitoring, A/B eval comparison) is warranted for systems where model quality has a direct impact on user experience or business outcomes. For internal tooling where quality is less critical, a simplified program (inventory + deprecation alerts + basic smoke tests) is sufficient.

The urgency of establishing the program increases with the number of models in use and the proximity of known deprecation dates. A team using a single model with no known EOS date can afford to defer formal program setup; a team using three models, one of which has an announced EOS in 8 months, needs the program running now.
Step 1: Create and maintain the model inventory

# config/model-inventory.yaml
# Tracks all model versions in use, their deprecation status,
# and the upgrade target when applicable.

models:
  - id: claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022
    provider: anthropic
    sdk_package: anthropic
    sdk_version_pinned: "0.40.0"
    use_cases: [agent_reasoning, tool_use, document_analysis]
    ga_date: "2024-10-22"
    eos_date: null          # not yet deprecated
    upgrade_target: null    # no upgrade planned
    eval_baseline_path: evals/baselines/claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022.json
    deployment_config: config/models/claude-3-5-sonnet.yaml

  - id: claude-3-haiku-20240307
    provider: anthropic
    sdk_package: anthropic
    sdk_version_pinned: "0.40.0"
    use_cases: [classification, short_response, routing]
    ga_date: "2024-03-07"
    eos_date: "2025-07-01"    # deprecated — migrate by 2025-04-01 (90 days before EOS)
    upgrade_target: claude-3-5-haiku-20241022
    eval_baseline_path: evals/baselines/claude-3-haiku-20240307.json
    migration_deadline: "2025-04-01"
    migration_status: in_progress

  - id: text-embedding-3-small
    provider: openai
    sdk_package: openai
    sdk_version_pinned: "1.50.0"
    use_cases: [retrieval_embedding]
    ga_date: "2024-01-25"
    eos_date: null
    upgrade_target: null
    eval_baseline_path: evals/baselines/retrieval-recall-text-embedding-3-small.json
Step 2: A/B eval comparison for model upgrades

# scripts/model-upgrade/compare-eval.py
"""
Runs the eval suite against two model versions and reports the delta.
Used during model version upgrade evaluation to assess quality impact.
"""
import json, os, statistics
from anthropic import Anthropic

def run_eval_suite(model: str, suite_path: str) -> dict:
    client = Anthropic()
    results = []
    with open(suite_path) as f:
        cases = json.load(f)
    for case in cases:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=case["max_tokens"],
            messages=case["messages"],
            tools=case.get("tools", []),
        )
        passed = evaluate_case(response, case)
        results.append({
            "case_id": case["id"],
            "passed": passed,
            "latency": response.usage.input_tokens + response.usage.output_tokens,
            "input_tokens": response.usage.input_tokens,
            "output_tokens": response.usage.output_tokens,
        })
    return {
        "model": model,
        "total": len(results),
        "passed": sum(1 for r in results if r["passed"]),
        "pass_rate": sum(1 for r in results if r["passed"]) / len(results),
        "avg_output_tokens": statistics.mean(r["output_tokens"] for r in results),
        "results": results,
    }

def evaluate_case(response, case: dict) -> bool:
    """Evaluate whether the response meets the case criteria."""
    expected = case.get("expected_criteria", {})
    if expected.get("has_tool_use"):
        tool_blocks = [b for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use"]
        if not tool_blocks:
            return False
        if "tool_name" in expected and tool_blocks[0].name != expected["tool_name"]:
            return False
    if expected.get("text_contains"):
        text = " ".join(b.text for b in response.content if b.type == "text")
        if expected["text_contains"].lower() not in text.lower():
            return False
    return True

def compare_models(current: str, candidate: str, suite_path: str) -> dict:
    print(f"Evaluating current model: {current}")
    current_results = run_eval_suite(current, suite_path)
    print(f"Evaluating candidate model: {candidate}")
    candidate_results = run_eval_suite(candidate, suite_path)

    pass_rate_delta = candidate_results["pass_rate"] - current_results["pass_rate"]
    token_delta_pct = (
        (candidate_results["avg_output_tokens"] - current_results["avg_output_tokens"])
        / current_results["avg_output_tokens"]
    )
    return {
        "current": current_results,
        "candidate": candidate_results,
        "pass_rate_delta": pass_rate_delta,
        "avg_output_token_delta_pct": token_delta_pct,
        "recommendation": "deploy" if pass_rate_delta >= -0.03 else "reject",
    }

# scripts/model-upgrade/sdk-compatibility-check.py
"""
Checks for breaking changes in a new LLM SDK version
by comparing response schemas against the pinned baseline.
"""
import json, importlib, sys
from packaging.version import Version

def check_anthropic_sdk_version(new_version: str) -> list[str]:
    """Returns list of potential breaking changes detected."""
    import anthropic
    current = Version(anthropic.__version__)
    target = Version(new_version)
    issues = []

    if target.major > current.major:
        issues.append(f"MAJOR version bump ({current} -> {target}): expect breaking API changes")

    # Check specific known breaking changes by version range
    KNOWN_BREAKING = [
        ("0.20", "0.21", "Streaming: RawContentBlockDeltaEvent.delta is now typed"),
        ("0.25", "0.26", "tool_use: stop_reason is now an enum, not a string"),
    ]
    for from_v, to_v, description in KNOWN_BREAKING:
        if Version(from_v) <= current < Version(to_v) <= target:
            issues.append(f"Known breaking change between {from_v} and {to_v}: {description}")

    return issues

def check_schema_compatibility(current_model: str, candidate_model: str) -> list[str]:
    """
    Runs a minimal API call with both models and compares the response schema.
    Detects field additions, removals, or type changes.
    """
    from anthropic import Anthropic
    client = Anthropic()

    def get_response_schema(model: str) -> dict:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=64,
            tools=[{"name": "test_tool", "description": "Test", "input_schema": {
                "type": "object", "properties": {"arg": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["arg"]
            }}],
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Call test_tool with arg='hello'"}],
        )
        return {
            "stop_reason_type": type(response.stop_reason).__name__,
            "content_types": sorted({b.type for b in response.content}),
            "usage_fields": sorted(response.usage.model_dump().keys()),
        }

    current_schema = get_response_schema(current_model)
    candidate_schema = get_response_schema(candidate_model)
    issues = []
    for field, current_val in current_schema.items():
        candidate_val = candidate_schema.get(field)
        if candidate_val != current_val:
            issues.append(f"Schema change in '{field}': {current_val!r} -> {candidate_val!r}")
    return issues

# scripts/model-upgrade/deprecation-monitor.py
"""
Monitors the model inventory for approaching deprecation deadlines
and creates Jira tickets for migration planning.
"""
from datetime import date, timedelta
import yaml, json
from pathlib import Path

INVENTORY_PATH = "config/model-inventory.yaml"
WARNING_DAYS = [90, 60, 30, 14]

def check_deprecations(today: date = None) -> list[dict]:
    today = today or date.today()
    inventory = yaml.safe_load(Path(INVENTORY_PATH).read_text())
    warnings = []
    for model in inventory["models"]:
        eos = model.get("eos_date")
        migration_deadline = model.get("migration_deadline")
        if not eos:
            continue
        eos_date = date.fromisoformat(eos)
        days_to_eos = (eos_date - today).days
        days_to_migration = None
        if migration_deadline:
            days_to_migration = (date.fromisoformat(migration_deadline) - today).days

        for threshold in WARNING_DAYS:
            if 0 < days_to_eos <= threshold:
                warnings.append({
                    "model_id": model["id"],
                    "eos_date": eos,
                    "days_to_eos": days_to_eos,
                    "days_to_migration": days_to_migration,
                    "upgrade_target": model.get("upgrade_target"),
                    "migration_status": model.get("migration_status", "not_started"),
                    "urgency": "critical" if days_to_eos <= 30 else "high" if days_to_eos <= 60 else "warning",
                })
                break
    return warnings

if __name__ == "__main__":
    warnings = check_deprecations()
    if warnings:
        print(f"Deprecation warnings: {len(warnings)}")
        for w in warnings:
            print(f"  [{w['urgency'].upper()}] {w['model_id']}: EOS in {w['days_to_eos']} days "
                  f"(target: {w['upgrade_target']} | status: {w['migration_status']})")
    else:
        print("No models approaching deprecation.")

SDK patch upgrade: anthropic SDK 0.39 to 0.40 (bug fix, no breaking changes noted in changelog). Triage: no breaking changes, no CVE — planned window, low priority. Process: run schema compatibility check (no differences found), run eval suite (pass rate unchanged), include in next monthly upgrade window alongside other low-risk patches. Update inventory SDK version after deployment.

Model version upgrade: evaluating claude-3-5-haiku as a replacement for claude-3-haiku in the classification use case. Triage: new capability, planned evaluation. Process: run A/B eval on 150-case classification eval suite. Results: claude-3-5-haiku pass rate 94.7% vs claude-3-haiku 91.2% (+3.5%), average output tokens 82 vs 94 (-13% — cost saving). Recommendation: deploy. Planned for next upgrade window with a 4-week parallel run (10% of classification traffic on new model) before full migration.

A customer support platform using claude-3-haiku for ticket routing and claude-3-5-sonnet for complex response drafting received a deprecation notice: claude-3-haiku would reach end-of-support in 7 months. The team had 90 days before their internal migration deadline (to complete migration 90 days before EOS).

They ran an A/B evaluation comparing claude-3-haiku against claude-3-5-haiku on their 200-case routing eval suite. Results: claude-3-5-haiku had 96.5% routing accuracy vs 93.0% for claude-3-haiku (the routing task was within claude-3-5-haiku's strength), but average latency was 340ms vs 210ms (62% slower — haiku-3.5 is larger). Since routing is latency-sensitive, the team configured a 500ms timeout with fallback to the rule-based router for cases where the model did not respond in time, preserving SLO compliance despite the latency increase.

The deployment followed the planned upgrade window process: staging deployment with eval comparison, 10% canary for 1 week (long canary due to latency concern), full rollout in week 3. Post-rollout, routing accuracy in production improved from 91% to 95% (matching the eval suite delta), and the timeout fallback triggered on 2.3% of routing requests — within the acceptable range.

Using model strings like 'claude-3-5-sonnet-latest' rather than 'claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022' means the deployed model version can change without a code or config deployment, making it impossible to reproduce production behaviour.
Eval suite that doesn't cover production task distribution:

An eval suite built from synthetic examples that don't reflect actual production inputs may show no regression on eval but regress on the production distribution. Build eval cases from real production inputs (anonymised if needed).

Assuming that a minor SDK version bump has no breaking changes because the changelog says 'bug fixes' — minor bumps sometimes include breaking changes that are not prominently documented. Always run the schema compatibility check.

Different model versions have different strengths and weaknesses for different task types. A model that is better on average may be worse for specific use cases. The eval suite must cover all use cases the model serves.
Not having a deprecation monitor means EOS dates are discovered from provider emails that may go unread. Set up automated monitoring with alerts at 90 days before migration deadline.
Pin all model version strings to dated identifiers:
Use 'claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022', not 'claude-3-5-sonnet-latest'. Store the pinned string in configuration, not in code, so it can be changed via config deployment without a code deploy.
Build the eval suite from production inputs:
Sample real production requests (anonymised) as eval cases. This ensures the eval distribution matches the production distribution and that regressions on real tasks are detected.
Run the A/B eval on a representative sample:
For cost reasons, the full A/B eval should run on a stratified sample of 100-200 cases covering all use case categories, not on the full production log.
Set migration deadlines at EOS minus 90 days:
This leaves 90 days of buffer for evaluation, window scheduling, and rollout. Do not allow migrations to compress into the final 30 days before EOS.

After a model upgrade, add the new model to the continuous eval monitoring pipeline. Alert if production eval quality drops more than 3% from the post-upgrade baseline, which may indicate a provider-side model change.

The A/B evaluation cost is the primary performance concern. Running 200 eval cases twice (current and candidate model) at approximately $0.001 per case costs $0.40 per evaluation run — negligible for most teams. For large eval suites (500+ cases) with expensive models, the cost may be $2-5 per run, which remains manageable.
The deprecation monitor script runs in under 10 seconds for a typical inventory of 5-15 models. It can be scheduled as a daily CI job or a Kubernetes CronJob with minimal resource requirements.

For teams managing upgrades across multiple products, the model inventory should be centralised (one YAML file per product, managed in a shared repository) rather than duplicated per service. This enables a centralised deprecation view across all products.
Pin version in requirements.txt; watch GitHub releases
Daily scan; alert on CRITICAL/HIGH in anthropic/openai
Subscribe to release feed of SDK repositories
Parameterised eval cases; JSON report for comparison
Ticket per upgrade with eval result attached
Daily alert on approaching EOS dates

The model inventory contains model version strings, provider account identifiers, and evaluation baselines. Ensure the inventory file has appropriate access controls: it should be readable by all engineers but writable only through a reviewed PR process.
Store eval baselines in version control with the commit hash of the code that generated them. This ensures that baseline comparisons are reproducible and cannot be tampered with.
API key security during eval runs:

The A/B evaluation makes direct API calls using the provider API key. Run eval comparisons in a sandboxed CI environment with a dedicated eval API key that has usage limits set to prevent runaway evaluation costs.
The rollback for a model version upgrade is a config change (revert the model string in the configuration file). Ensure this can be deployed within 30 minutes of detecting a production regression.

If a provider API key is rotated or revoked, the running agent loses its LLM capability entirely. Maintain a backup provider configuration (e.g., a fallback to a different provider or model) and test the fallback path quarterly.

Think of the model version upgrade program as the maintenance schedule for a high-precision instrument. A laboratory instrument needs calibration checks (eval baseline comparison), part replacement on a schedule (planned upgrade windows), monitoring for degradation (continuous eval quality monitoring), and advance planning when a part manufacturer announces end-of-production (deprecation management). Running the instrument without these maintenance practices means you only discover degradation when a measurement fails.

The eval suite is the calibration check: it tells you whether the instrument (the model) is still performing to specification after a change. The model inventory is the parts manifest: it tells you what you have, when you got it, and when you need to replace it. The deprecation monitor is the manufacturer notification: it tells you when to start planning for a replacement before supply runs out.
Pin all model version strings to dated identifiers ('claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022', not 'latest') and store them in configuration, not code, to enable config-driven migration.
Maintain a model inventory YAML that tracks every model in use, its EOS date, the upgrade target, and the evaluation baseline path — this is the source of truth for the upgrade program.
Run an A/B eval comparison between the current and candidate model before every model version upgrade; a 3% or greater pass rate drop is a rejection criterion.
Set the internal migration deadline at EOS minus 90 days; automated deprecation alerts at 90, 60, 30, and 14 days before the migration deadline prevent last-minute scrambling.
SDK version upgrades require API schema compatibility checks in addition to the eval suite; SDK changelogs do not always document breaking response format changes.
Build the eval suite from real (anonymised) production inputs to ensure that the eval distribution reflects the actual production task distribution.
